description: Nigerian party fried rice — colourful, fragrant, loaded with liver, mixed vegetables, and seasoned with curry and thyme. Not Chinese fried rice. This is its own thing entirely and it demands respect.

ingredients.name: parboiled long grain rice
ingredients.note: Cook the rice separately first — plain, slightly undercooked. Fried rice is always made with pre-cooked rice. Hot freshly cooked rice turns mushy. Ideally cook the rice the day before and refrigerate overnight.

ingredients.name: chicken liver
ingredients.note: The defining protein of Nigerian fried rice. Cook separately in seasoned butter or oil before adding. Do not skip — it is what makes it Nigerian fried rice.

ingredients.name: chicken stock
ingredients.note: Used to cook the rice initially and as the liquid base. Rich stock only — this is where the flavour of the rice comes from.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: For frying everything. Some cooks use a mix of oil and butter for richness.

ingredients.name: butter
ingredients.note: For cooking the liver and adds richness. Margarine is a common Nigerian substitute.

ingredients.name: carrots
ingredients.note: Classic. Cut to a uniform small dice — about 5mm. They should stay slightly firm in the final rice.

ingredients.name: green beans
ingredients.note: Slice at an angle, thinly. Do not overcook — they should have a slight bite.

ingredients.name: green pepper (capsicum)
ingredients.note: Adds colour, freshness, and a slight bitterness that balances the richness.

ingredients.name: sweet corn
ingredients.note: Adds sweetness and colour contrast. Canned — drain well. Frozen — thaw first.

ingredients.name: green peas
ingredients.note: Thaw before use. Classic Nigerian fried rice component.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for cooking the rice in stock, 1 finely diced for frying in the rice.

ingredients.name: spring onions (scallions)
ingredients.note: Slice finely. Add at the very end — they are a finishing ingredient, not a cooking one.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: This is what gives Nigerian fried rice its colour and its identity. Do not reduce.

ingredients.name: thyme
ingredients.note: Part of the classic Nigerian spice pairing with curry.

ingredients.name: soy sauce
ingredients.note: A small amount adds depth and a touch of colour without making it taste Chinese. Optional but recommended.

ingredients.name: white pepper
ingredients.note: For the liver and the rice. Adds warmth.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season carefully — the stock, seasoning cubes, and soy sauce all contribute salt.

steps.title: Cook the Rice in Stock
steps.instruction: The rice is the foundation of this dish and it needs to be prepared well in advance — ideally the night before. Bring the chicken stock to a boil in a large pot and add 1 roughly chopped onion, the curry powder, thyme, and 1 crushed seasoning cube — this is where the rice gets its golden colour and its savoury base flavour. Wash the parboiled rice until the water runs fairly clear, then add it to the boiling seasoned stock and stir once to prevent sticking. Cook on medium heat, covered, until the rice has absorbed all the stock and is just barely cooked through — it should be slightly firm and each grain should be distinct, not soft or sticky. Spread the cooked rice immediately onto a wide tray or baking sheet in a thin layer and leave to cool completely before refrigerating for at least 1 hour. Overnight is strongly preferred.
micro_steps: Pour 3 cups of chicken stock into a large pot and bring to a boil over high heat
Add 1 roughly chopped onion, 1½ tsp curry powder, 1 tsp thyme, and 1 crushed seasoning cube
Stir the stock and let it return to a rolling boil
Wash the parboiled rice under cold water until the water runs fairly clear
Add the washed rice to the boiling stock and stir once
Reduce heat to medium, cover the pot, and cook until all the stock is absorbed — about 15–18 minutes
Check at 15 minutes: the rice should be just barely cooked with a very slight firmness — not soft, not crunchy
Remove from heat immediately when all liquid is absorbed
Spread the rice in a thin, even layer on a wide tray or baking sheet
Leave uncovered at room temperature until fully cooled — at least 30 minutes
Transfer to the fridge and refrigerate for at least 1 hour — overnight is strongly preferred
The rice is ready to fry when it is completely cold and the grains have dried out slightly
prep_notes: Cook the rice the night before if at all possible — cold, day-old rice fries dramatically better than freshly cooked rice
Use proper, rich chicken stock for cooking the rice — this is where most of the flavour lives and watery stock produces bland fried rice regardless of what you add later
Dice all vegetables — carrots, green beans, green pepper — to a uniform 5mm size before starting any cooking
Thaw the peas and sweet corn completely and drain them thoroughly before the frying stage begins
steps.sensory_cue: The cooked rice should be a beautiful, even golden-yellow colour from the curry and stock — vivid but not garish. Every grain should be separate and slightly firm when pressed between two fingers, with no stickiness or clumping. It should smell savoury, fragrant, and distinctly Nigerian. After chilling, the grains will feel dry and separate — this is exactly right.
steps.failure_risk: Hot, freshly cooked rice thrown into a wok turns into a sticky, clumped mass almost instantly — the excess moisture causes the grains to break down and glue together. Cold, slightly undercooked rice is the non-negotiable foundation of good fried rice. If you cannot refrigerate overnight, at minimum spread it on a tray and leave it uncovered in the fridge for at least 2 hours before frying.

steps.title: Cook the Liver
steps.instruction: Chicken liver is the heart of Nigerian fried rice and it is also the most technically demanding component — it overcooks in a matter of seconds and the difference between perfect and ruined is a very narrow window. Wash the liver thoroughly, removing any visible green bile spots or membrane, then cut into small, uniform cubes of about 1cm — uniform size is important for even cooking. Season with white pepper, a pinch of curry powder, salt, and a tiny pinch of thyme. Heat the butter and a small splash of oil together in a pan over the highest heat you have until the butter foams and the foam subsides. Add the liver in a single, uncrowded layer and fry for exactly 2 minutes per side until the outside is deeply browned but the interior is still faintly pink when you cut one open. Remove the liver from the pan immediately — it will finish cooking gently from residual heat and again when added to the hot rice.
micro_steps: Wash the chicken liver under cold running water — remove any visible green spots (bile) by cutting them away
Pull off any visible membrane or connecting tissue
Cut the liver into uniform 1cm cubes — consistent size ensures even cooking
Place in a bowl and season with ½ tsp white pepper, a pinch of curry powder, a small pinch of salt, and a pinch of thyme
Toss to coat evenly
Heat 2 tbsp butter and 1 tbsp oil together in a pan over high heat
Wait until the butter foams, then the foam subsides — this is when the temperature is right
Add the liver in a single uncrowded layer — work in batches if needed
Fry undisturbed for exactly 2 minutes — do not stir or move
Flip each piece and fry the other side for exactly 2 minutes
Cut one piece open — the outside should be deeply browned, the inside still faintly pink
Remove the liver to a plate immediately — do not leave it in the hot pan
steps.sensory_cue: Correctly cooked liver should be deeply and evenly browned on all sides with a slight firmness when pressed — not soft and squishy, not hard and grey. When you cut one open, the interior should be a pale pink, not red and raw, not grey and uniform throughout. It should smell rich, deeply savoury, and slightly mineral — not grey or steamed.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooked liver is the most common Nigerian fried rice failure and the most complained-about result. It happens because the heat is too low (liver steams instead of sears and goes grey) or the pan is crowded (temperature drops and the same result). High heat, small batches, and stopping while still slightly pink inside are the three rules. The liver finishes cooking when added to the hot rice — if it looks perfect before going in, it will be overdone by the end.

steps.title: Blanch the Vegetables
steps.instruction: Bring a small pot of well-salted water to a hard, rolling boil. Add the diced carrots first and cook for exactly 2 minutes — they need slightly longer than the beans because they are denser. Add the sliced green beans and cook for exactly 1 more minute. Drain everything immediately into a colander, then plunge into a bowl of very cold water with ice if you have it — this shock stops the cooking instantly and locks in the bright, vivid colours. Leave in the cold water for 2 minutes, drain thoroughly, and pat dry. Have all remaining vegetables — green pepper, sweet corn, peas — also fully drained and dried before the frying stage begins. Wet vegetables steam in the wok instead of frying.
micro_steps: Fill a small pot with water, add a generous pinch of salt, and bring to a hard rolling boil
Prepare a large bowl of cold water — add ice cubes if available
Add the diced carrots to the boiling water
Set a timer for 2 minutes — do not go over
At the 2-minute mark, add the sliced green beans
Cook for exactly 1 more minute
Drain immediately into a colander
Transfer immediately to the cold water bath — submerge completely
Leave in the cold water for 2 minutes
Drain the vegetables thoroughly
Spread on paper towels and pat completely dry
Ensure sweet corn is drained and peas are fully thawed and dry — spread on paper towels if needed
steps.sensory_cue: Blanched and shocked carrots should be a vivid, bright orange — not pale or faded. Green beans should be a vivid, almost fluorescent bright green — the cold water shock is what sets this colour permanently. Both should snap cleanly when bent rather than bending limply. If the colours look dull or faded, they were over-blanched.
steps.failure_risk: Skipping the cold water plunge after blanching allows residual heat in the vegetables to keep cooking them — by the time they reach the wok they are already overcooked and will turn mushy, grey, and flavourless in the final rice. The cold shock is not optional if you want vivid, crisp vegetables. Also make sure everything is fully dry before frying — wet vegetables release steam in the wok and make everything soggy.

steps.title: First Fry — Aromatics and Vegetables
steps.instruction: Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in your largest wok or widest, heaviest pan over the absolute highest heat your stove produces — this is the most critical technical requirement of the entire recipe. Add the finely diced onion and it should sizzle loudly and aggressively the moment it hits the oil. Stir-fry vigorously for 2 minutes until translucent and starting to colour at the edges. Add the diced green pepper and stir-fry for 1 minute. Add the blanched carrots and green beans, then the sweet corn and peas. Stir-fry everything together vigorously for 2 minutes — keep the spatula moving constantly, tossing and folding rather than just pushing. Add the soy sauce if using and season with a small pinch of salt.
micro_steps: Heat 2 tablespoons of vegetable oil in a large wok or wide heavy pan over the highest heat possible
Wait until the oil shimmers and the first wisps of smoke appear — this is the correct temperature
Add the finely diced onion — it should sizzle loudly and immediately
Stir-fry vigorously for 2 minutes, keeping the onion moving constantly
Add the diced green pepper and stir-fry for 1 minute
Add the blanched carrots and green beans — stir-fry for 1 minute
Add the sweet corn and peas — stir-fry for 1 minute
Keep everything moving constantly — toss and fold, do not just push things around
Add 1 tablespoon of soy sauce if using — it will sizzle and release steam immediately
Add a small pinch of salt
Stir-fry everything together for 1 more minute
The vegetables should look glossy, slightly charred at the edges, and vividly colourful
steps.sensory_cue: Everything in the wok should be sizzling aggressively — a loud, constant sound, not quiet or intermittent. The vegetables should look glossy, vividly coloured, and slightly charred at the edges from the high heat. The kitchen should smell hot, savoury, and slightly smoky — the smell of wok cooking at high temperature is distinctive and tells you the heat is right.
steps.failure_risk: Insufficient heat is the enemy of good fried rice at every stage. Low heat means the vegetables steam rather than fry, losing their vivid colours, their texture, and the characteristic smoky wok aroma that is part of what makes Nigerian fried rice special. The heat must be genuinely high — as high as your stove will go — throughout this step and the next.

steps.title: Add Rice and Fry
steps.instruction: Add all the cold, pre-cooked rice to the wok with the vegetables in one go. Use a spatula or wooden spoon to immediately break up any clumps, pressing them against the side of the wok — you want every grain separated and in contact with the hot oil and vegetables. Toss and fold the rice continuously over high heat, working quickly and keeping everything moving. Add the remaining crushed seasoning cube. If the rice looks pale rather than the vivid golden-yellow it should be, add a small pinch more curry powder and toss in. Fry for 4–5 minutes total, tossing constantly, until every grain is hot, separated, evenly coloured, and coated with the vegetable flavours.
micro_steps: Add all the cold rice to the wok in one go
Immediately start breaking up clumps with the spatula — press against the side of the wok
Begin tossing and folding the rice with the vegetables continuously
Crush the remaining seasoning cube to powder and sprinkle over the rice — toss in
Assess the colour — the rice should be a vivid golden-yellow throughout
If any sections look pale, add a small pinch of curry powder directly onto those areas and toss in
Keep the rice moving constantly — toss and fold, do not let it sit on the heat
Fry for 4–5 minutes total, tossing throughout
After 4 minutes, press a small handful of rice flat in the wok and check — every grain should be separate
The rice is ready when it is uniformly golden, hot all the way through, and each grain moves freely
steps.sensory_cue: The rice should look vibrant golden-yellow with colourful vegetable pieces evenly distributed throughout — a vivid mix of orange, green, and yellow. Every grain should be completely separate, not clumping. The wok should be producing wisps of steam and a slightly smoky, toasted rice aroma — that slightly nutty, hot smell is the signature of rice that has been properly fried rather than just warmed through.
steps.failure_risk: Stopping or slowing the stirring even briefly at high heat will cause the rice to stick and burn in patches on the bottom of the wok. Keep the spatula moving constantly. If you feel resistance and the rice is beginning to stick, drizzle a tiny amount of oil around the inner edges of the wok and toss immediately — this usually rescues it.

steps.title: Add Liver, Finish and Serve
steps.instruction: Add the cooked liver pieces to the rice and vegetables. Fold them in gently with a spatula using a lifting and turning motion — do not stir vigorously or the liver cubes will break into crumbles. Add the sliced spring onions and fold in with the same gentle motion. Taste the rice now — adjust salt if needed, adding in small pinches and tasting between each addition. Fry together for 1 final minute to heat everything through, then remove the wok from the heat immediately. Plate and serve straight away — Nigerian fried rice does not hold well and the quality degrades noticeably within minutes of coming off the heat.
micro_steps: Add the cooked liver pieces to the rice
Use a spatula to fold the liver in gently — lift and turn rather than stir and push
Add the sliced spring onions and fold in the same way
Taste the rice — assess the salt level carefully
Add salt in the smallest possible pinches, tasting between each addition
Fry together for exactly 1 minute, folding gently throughout
Remove the wok from the heat at the 1-minute mark — do not leave it on
Plate immediately onto a serving dish
Serve within 5 minutes — the quality drops noticeably the longer it sits
steps.sensory_cue: The finished rice should be a vivid, colourful landscape — golden rice, bright orange carrot, vivid green beans and peas, yellow corn, dark liver pieces, and the bright green of fresh spring onion scattered throughout. The smell should be fragrant, savoury, and deeply appetising. One forkful should deliver multiple textures simultaneously — firm separate grains of rice, slightly firm vegetables, tender liver, and the fresh bite of spring onion.
steps.failure_risk: Leaving the rice on the heat after the liver and spring onions are added dries it out rapidly, overcooks the liver from perfectly tender to grainy and chalky, and wilts the spring onion from fresh and bright to limp and flavourless. Off the heat and onto the plate the moment it is done — Nigerian fried rice waits for no one.
